title: The {key:[} and {key:]} Family
subtitle: {key:[} goes back, {key:]} goes forward. The semantic siblings of search.
summary: [ and ] form a paired-prefix family for navigating semantically: previous/next bracket, function, comment, change, spelling error, etc. Mostly used in code.

blocks.text: The bracket-prefix family is *symmetric*: {key:[} always means "backward," {key:]} always means "forward." The second key picks the *thing* to look for. {key:[}{key:c} = previous diff hunk; {key:]}{key:c} = next diff hunk. {key:[}{key:m} = previous method start; {key:]}{key:m} = next.

blocks.title: Symmetry Is the Whole Idea
blocks.text:
Once you internalize the convention, the entire family becomes guessable:

  • {key:[}{key:(} / {key:]}{key:)} — previous / next unmatched paren.
  • {key:[}{key:{} / {key:]}{key:}} — previous / next unmatched brace.
  • {key:[}{key:m} / {key:]}{key:m} — previous / next *method start*.
  • {key:[}{key:M} / {key:]}{key:M} — previous / next *method end*.
  • {key:[}{key:#} / {key:]}{key:#} — previous / next preprocessor `#if`/`#endif`.
  • {key:[}{key:s} / {key:]}{key:s} — previous / next spelling error.
  • {key:[}{key:c} / {key:]}{key:c} — previous / next diff change. *(Diff mode itself is out of scope here; `:help diff` is the entry point.)*
  • {key:[}{key:I} — list lines containing the keyword under cursor (powerful, obscure).

Most are language-aware via filetype plugins. {key:[}{key:m} on a Java buffer finds method starts; on plain text it does nothing useful.

rows: List occurrences of keyword under cursor

blocks.title: Bracket-prefix commands are 'navigate by structure'
blocks.text: The {key:[} and {key:]} prefixes share a theme: jump to the next/previous structural token. {key:[}{key:[}, {key:]}{key:]} jump by section; {key:[}{key:m}, {key:]}{key:m} by method; {key:[}{key:s}, {key:]}{key:s} by misspelled word; {key:[}{key:c}, {key:]}{key:c} by diff hunk. Each pair shares the same direction-flip implementation; only the structural predicate changes.
